case_name: Satvinder Kaur v. State (Govt. of NCT of Delhi)
key_held: A woman has the right to lodge complaint/FIR from any place other than where the incident occurred. Police cannot refuse registration on grounds of territorial jurisdiction.
notes: Precursor to Zero FIR. Affirmed by SC. Codified in BNSS Sec 173(1).

case_name: Lalita Kumari v. Govt. of U.P.
key_held: Registration of FIR is mandatory if information discloses commission of cognizable offence. No preliminary enquiry unless case falls in exceptions.
notes: Landmark SC but extensively relied by all High Courts. BNSS retains mandatory registration.

case_name: DBS Bank v. State of Kerala (Vatakara PS)
key_held: Purpose of Zero FIR under BNSS is to ensure victims can file complaints regardless of jurisdiction. Police cannot refuse FIR on territorial grounds even if part of offence outside PS limits.
notes: Direct application of BNSS 173 Zero FIR. Directed Magistrate to reconsider.

case_name: Kirti Vashisht v. State
key_held: Zero FIR must be registered irrespective of jurisdiction especially in crimes against women. Police must transfer to jurisdictional PS within 24 hours.
notes: Post Nirbhaya emphasis. Delhi Police circulars issued based on it.

case_name: Anurag Bhatnagar v. State (NCT of Delhi)
key_held: Magistrate should not entertain direct Sec 156(3) application unless complainant first exhausted police remedies (SHO then SP). But procedural irregularity only.
notes: 2025 ruling reinforcing hierarchy. High Courts now follow strictly for BNSS.

case_name: Moti Singh Sikarwar v. State of U.P.
key_held: Cognizance of offence under IPC 174A (non-appearance after summons) can be taken only on written complaint by Court. Police cannot lodge FIR suo motu.
notes: Clarifies bar on police FIR in certain cases. Relevant for BNSS transition.

case_name: Annapurna Devi v. State of U.P.
key_held: Magistrate must fix timeframe (1-2 weeks) for police to register FIR when ordering u/s 156(3) CrPC.
notes: Allahabad HC Circular issued. Still followed in BNSS applications.

case_name: State v. Ranjit Singh
key_held: Electronic FIR (e-FIR) valid under new laws. Signature within 3 days rule under BNSS 173 must be strictly followed.
notes: Early interpretation of BNSS e-FIR provisions.

case_name: Victim X v. State of Maharashtra
key_held: Preliminary enquiry under BNSS Sec 173(3) limited to 3-7 yr offences. DSP permission mandatory; cannot be used to delay registration.
notes: Clarifies preliminary enquiry scope post BNSS.

case_name: Priyanka Srivastava v. State of U.P.
key_held: Application u/s 156(3) must be supported by affidavit. Complainant must show steps taken u/s 154(1) & (3).
notes: Widely followed by High Courts. Affidavit requirement persists.

case_name: Arnesh Kumar v. State of Bihar
key_held: Police must not arrest routinely in <7 year offences (esp. 498A). Must record reasons per CrPC 41. Notice u/s 41A preferred.
chapter_name: Arrest, Detention & Police Powers (BNSS Sec 35, CrPC 41)
notes: Landmark. High Courts issue guidelines for police. BNSS 35 adopts similar.

case_name: D.K. Basu v. State of West Bengal
key_held: 11 mandatory guidelines for arrest & detention: inform grounds, right to lawyer, medical exam, family intimation etc.
chapter_name: Arrest, Detention & Police Powers (BNSS Sec 35, CrPC 41)
notes: Constitutional. All High Courts enforce strictly. BNSS incorporates.

case_name: Joginder Kumar v. State of U.P.
key_held: FIR registration does not automatically warrant arrest. Arrest must be justified; mere suspicion insufficient.
chapter_name: Arrest, Detention & Police Powers (BNSS Sec 35, CrPC 41)
notes: Core principle repeated by High Courts.

case_name: Delhi Judicial Services Association v. State of Gujarat
key_held: Guidelines for arrest of judicial officers: prior intimation to District Judge/High Court, no immediate police station visit.
chapter_name: Arrest, Detention & Police Powers (BNSS Sec 35, CrPC 41)
notes: Protects independence of judiciary. Followed nationwide.

case_name: Sheela Barse v. State of Maharashtra
key_held: Magistrate must inform arrested person of right to medical examination u/s 54 CrPC.
chapter_name: Arrest, Detention & Police Powers (BNSS Sec 35, CrPC 41)

case_name: State of Haryana v. Dinesh Kumar
key_held: Distinction between arrest and custody explained. Arrest is specific act; custody broader.
chapter_name: Arrest, Detention & Police Powers (BNSS Sec 35, CrPC 41)
notes: Important for bail and police powers.

case_name: Vinubhai Haribhai Malviya v. State of Gujarat
key_held: Criminal investigation must uphold Article 21. Fair process for accused and victim.
chapter_name: Arrest, Detention & Police Powers (BNSS Sec 35, CrPC 41)

case_name: Ishwar Pratap Singh v. State of U.P.
key_held: Criminal investigation is exclusive police domain. Courts should not supervise unless exceptional.
chapter_name: Arrest, Detention & Police Powers (BNSS Sec 35, CrPC 41)

case_name: Mukesh Singh v. State (NCT of Delhi)
key_held: Compelling accused to attend TIP does not violate Art 20(3).
chapter_name: Arrest, Detention & Police Powers (BNSS Sec 35, CrPC 41)

case_name: Bombay HC on Arrest in 498A cases
key_held: Police must follow Arnesh Kumar strictly; no mechanical arrest in matrimonial cases.
chapter_name: Arrest, Detention & Police Powers (BNSS Sec 35, CrPC 41)

case_name: Shreya Singhal v. Union of India
key_held: Section 66A IT Act struck down as unconstitutional (vague, overbroad). Violates Art 19(1)(a). Sec 69A & 79 upheld with conditions.
notes: Landmark. All High Courts now refuse 66A cases. Safe harbour clarified.

case_name: Avnish Bajaj v. State (Bazee.com case)
key_held: CEO of intermediary liable for obscene material uploaded by user? (Bail granted; later settled).
notes: Early cyber intermediary liability case. Pre-79 amendment.

case_name: Syed Asifuddin v. State of A.P.
key_held: Tampering with mobile ESN (electronic serial number) amounts to tampering with computer source document u/s 65 IT Act.

case_name: Suhas Katti v. State
key_held: First conviction under Sec 67 IT Act (obscene messages). Convicted for posting defamatory messages about divorced woman.
notes: Historic first IT Act conviction in India (7 months trial).

case_name: State of Tamil Nadu v. Suhas Katti
key_held: Conviction under 67 IT Act, 469 & 509 IPC for online harassment via Yahoo groups.

case_name: Poona Auto Ancillaries v. PNB
key_held: Bank held negligent; compensation of Rs.45 lakh awarded for phishing fraud under IT Act 43A.

case_name: Vyakti Vikas Kendra v. Google (Art of Living case)
key_held: Intermediary Google directed to remove defamatory blog posts within 36 hours.

case_name: Frios v. State of Kerala
key_held: Upheld validity of protected system notification u/s 70 IT Act for FRIENDS software.

case_name: Kumar v. Whiteley (JANET hacking)
key_held: Unauthorized access + alteration of data amounts to hacking (influenced Indian IT Act 66).
notes: Comparative law used by Indian HCs.

case_name: Anvar P.V. v. P.K. Basheer
key_held: Electronic records (secondary evidence) admissible only if Sec 65B Evidence Act certificate conditions satisfied.
notes: Critical for digital evidence in all High Courts.

case_name: Harpal Singh v. State
key_held: 65B certificate requirements for CCTV, call records etc.

case_name: R v. Gold & Schifreen
key_held: Mere unauthorized access without damage not always criminal (influenced Indian law).

case_name: Kshipra Uke & Shiv Shankar Das v. State of Maharashtra
key_held: Intellectual property, research data, laptops are 'property' under SC/ST POA. Theft of digital research material = atrocity. Compensation payable.
notes: Landmark expansion of 'property'. Upheld by SC 2025.

case_name: Supreme Court upholds Bombay HC SC/ST IP verdict
key_held: Affirmed Bombay HC: intangible property like IP qualifies for relief under Atrocities Act.

case_name: Ashabai v. State of Maharashtra
key_held: Complainant need not disclose caste of accused in complaint under SC/ST POA for registration.

case_name: Bombay HC on video recording in SC/ST bail
key_held: Even if bail proceedings in open court, video recording mandatory in SC/ST POA cases.

case_name: National Commission SC directions implementation
key_held: High Courts direct implementation of NCSC recommendations for compensation & SIT in atrocity cases.

case_name: Exclusive Special Courts mandate
key_held: States directed to establish Exclusive Special Courts u/s 14 SC/ST POA for speedy trial.

case_name: No anticipatory bail in SC/ST (Sec 18)
key_held: Sec 18 SC/ST POA bars anticipatory bail. High Courts follow strictly unless prima facie no offence.

case_name: Victim compensation & rights
key_held: State must provide relief including travel, medical, legal aid under SC/ST Rules.

case_name: Bachan Singh v. State of Punjab
key_held: Death penalty only in 'rarest of rare' cases. High Courts apply sentencing guidelines.

case_name: Tukaram v. State of Maharashtra (Mathura Rape)
key_held: Controversial; led to major rape law reforms. Consent issues.

case_name: Nirbhaya case (Mukesh v. NCT Delhi)
key_held: Confirmed death for gangrape-murder. Led to 2013 amendments.

case_name: State of Maharashtra v. M.H. George
key_held: Mens rea presumed unless statute indicates otherwise.

case_name: KM Nanavati v. State of Maharashtra
key_held: Burden of proof on prosecution; general exceptions explained.

case_name: Mahboob Shah v. Emperor
key_held: Distinction between common intention (Sec 34 IPC) and similar intention.

case_name: Barendra Kumar Ghosh v. Emperor
key_held: Mere presence + shared intention sufficient for liability u/s 34.

case_name: Rajiv Gandhi assassination (State of TN v. Nalini)
key_held: Mere association/knowledge not enough for conspiracy. Meeting of minds required.

case_name: State (NCT Delhi) v. Shiv Charan Bansal
key_held: Criminal conspiracy proved by cumulative circumstantial evidence.

case_name: Kedar Nath Singh v. State of Bihar
key_held: Sedition (124A) valid as reasonable restriction. (Now repealed in BNS, spirit in 152).

case_name: Shatrughan Chauhan v. UOI
key_held: Mental illness as supervening circumstance for commuting death sentence.

case_name: Accused X v. State of Maharashtra
key_held: Post-conviction mental illness is mitigating factor for death penalty.

case_name: Anita Thakur v. State of J&K
key_held: Right to peaceful protest; police force only if assembly turns violent.

case_name: Anuradha Bhasin v. Union of India
key_held: Sec 144 orders must balance rights with proportionality. Less intrusive measures preferred.

case_name: State of Orissa v. Sharat Chandra Sahu
key_held: Police can investigate non-cognizable offence arising from same facts as cognizable.

case_name: Nand Lal v. State of Chhattisgarh
key_held: Immediate FIR crucial in group clashes to avoid suspicion of over-implication.

case_name: State of U.P. v. Raghuvir
key_held: Unexplained delay in FIR viewed with suspicion; courts scrutinize.

case_name: Satpal v. State of Haryana
key_held: FIR is not encyclopedia; need not contain every detail.

case_name: Sharad Hiru Kolambe v. State of Maharashtra
key_held: Default sentences for non-payment of fine must be in addition (not concurrent).

case_name: Sanjeev Kapoor v. Chandana Kapoor
key_held: Court not functus officio after maintenance order; can alter u/s 127 CrPC.

case_name: Allahabad HC on public servant FIR
key_held: No prior sanction needed for registration of FIR against public servant. Sanction at cognizance stage.

case_name: Kerala HC on Zero FIR transfer
key_held: Zero FIR to be transferred to jurisdictional PS within 24 hours. Police HQ circulars.

case_name: Madras HC on e-evidence in cyber cases
key_held: Strict compliance with 65B for WhatsApp chats, emails in IT Act cases.

case_name: Delhi HC on preliminary enquiry in BNSS
key_held: Preliminary enquiry (BNSS 173(3)) only for 3-7 yr cases; 14 days max with DSP approval.

case_name: Bombay HC on victim rights in FIR
key_held: Free copy of FIR mandatory; police must provide immediately to victim.
